24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	  
148	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
149	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
150	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
151	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
152	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
153	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
154	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
155	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
156	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
157	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
158	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
159	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
160	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
161	\end{proof}
162	
163	\section{Projective dimension}
164	\label{sec:ab_pd}
165	
166	\begin{definition}
167	
168	\leanok
169	  [Projective dimension]
170	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
171	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
172	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
173	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
174	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
175	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
176	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
177	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
178	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
179	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
180	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
181	  \[
182	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
183	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
184	  \]
185	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
186	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
187	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
188	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
189	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
190	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
191	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
192	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
193	\end{definition}
194	
195	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
196	\begin{itemize}
197	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
198	    iff \(M\) is free);
199	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
200	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
201	\end{itemize}
202	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
203	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
204	
205	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
206	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
207	
208	\begin{lemma}
209	
210	\leanok
211	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
212	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
213	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
214	  
215	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
216	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
217	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
218	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
219	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
220	  %  \begin{enumerate}
221	  %  \item
222	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
223	  %  \item
224	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
225	  %  \item
226	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
227	  %  \end{enumerate}"
228	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
229	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
230	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
231	  \(R\)-modules. Then
232	  \begin{enumerate}
233	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
234	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
235	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
236	  \end{enumerate}
237	\end{lemma}
238	
239	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
240	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
241	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
242	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
243	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
244	%  from Lemma REF."
245	\begin{proof}
246	  
247	  
248	  
249	  By Lemma~REF, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
250	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
251	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
252	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
253	  \[
254	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
255	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
256	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
257	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
258	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
259	  \]
260	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
261	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
262	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
263	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
264	\end{proof}
265	
266	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
267	\label{sec:ab_main}
268	
269	\begin{theorem}
270	
271	\leanok
272	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
273	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
274	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
275	  
276	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
277	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
278	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
279	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
280	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
281	  %  \(\)
282	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
283	  %  \(\)"
284	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
285	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
286	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
287	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
288	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
289	  \[
290	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
291	  \]
292	\end{theorem}
293	
294	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
295	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting
296	%  case is the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\). In this case, let
297	%  \(\)
298	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
299	%  \(\)
300	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so \(e = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
301	%  By Lemma REF we may assume all matrix
302	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
303	%  ideal of \(R\). Then on the one hand, by
304	%  Proposition REF we see that
305	%  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, breaking the long
306	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
307	%  \begin{align*}
308	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
309	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
310	%  \ldots,\\
311	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
312	%  \end{align*}
313	%  we see, using Lemma REF, that
314	%  \begin{align*}
315	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
316	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
317	%  \ldots,\\
318	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
319	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
320	%  \end{align*}
321	%  and since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we conclude \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\).
322	%  This finishes the proof of the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).
323	%
324	%  Induction step. If \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), then we pick \(x \in \mathfrak m\)
325	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both \(M\) and \(R\). This is possible, because
326	%  either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\) and \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the aforementioned
327	%  Proposition REF or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which
328	%  case \(M\) is finite free hence also \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\).
329	%  Thus \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by Lemma REF
330	%  (for example) and we can find an \(x \in \mathfrak m\) which is a nonzerodivisor
331	%  on both \(R\) and \(M\). Let
332	%  \(\)
333	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
334	%  \(\)
335	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
336	%  shows that
337	%  \(\)
338	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
339	%  M/xM \to 0
340	%  \(\)
341	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\).
342	%  By Lemma REF we have
343	%  \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
344	%  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\).
345	%  Till now depths have all been depths as \(R\) modules, but we observe that
346	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
347	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
348	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
349	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective
350	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
351	\begin{proof}
352	  
353	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
354	
355	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
356	  finite free resolution
357	  \[
358	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
359	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
360	  \]
361	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
362	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
363	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
364	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
365	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
366	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
367	  sequences
368	  \[
369	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
370	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
371	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
372	  \]
373	  and applying Lemma~REF iteratively,
374	  yields the chain of inequalities
375	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
376	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
377	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
378	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
379	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
380	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
381	
382	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
383	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
384	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
385	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
386	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
387	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
388	  Lemma~REF, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
389	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
390	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
391	  \[
392	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
393	  \]
394	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
395	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
396	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
397	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
398	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
399	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
400	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
401	  \[
402	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
403	  \]
404	  Substituting yields
405	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
406	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
407	\end{proof}

409	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
410	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
411	
412	\begin{definition}
413	
414	\leanok
415	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
416	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
417	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
418	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
419	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
420	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
421	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
422	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
423	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
424	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
425	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
426	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
427	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
428	\end{definition}
429	
430	% NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-184): the Lean assembly
431	% `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` calls a non-private helper
432	% `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
433	% L1096 — line drifted from iter-184's L944 reference) that now carries a
434	% structural induction scaffold via `regularLocal_inductive_step`, with the
435	% load-bearing sorry pushed into two named helpers: (i)
436	% `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` consolidating the
437	% Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate gaps in one signature, and (ii) an inline
438	% technical-bridge sorry inside `regularLocal_inductive_step` for the
439	% `R⧸(x)`-vs-`QuotSMulTop x R` linear-equiv upgrade (~10-20 LOC iter-186 close
440	% via `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` + `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`).
441	% Neither has a `\lean{...}` pin in this chapter (the lean-vs-blueprint-checker
442	% iter185-auslander minor flagged adding a pin for the public-facing
443	% `exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` itself; iter-186+ chapter touch can add
444	% that hint). The Stacks 00NQ Mathlib gap remains the load-bearing substrate;
445	% iter-186+ work paths unchanged: (a) formalise Stacks 00NQ in project
446	% (~300 LOC); (b) Koszul-homology bypass via `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`;
447	% (c) Mathlib upstream.
448	\begin{corollary}
449	
450	\leanok
451	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
452	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
453	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
454	  
455	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
456	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
457	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
458	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
459	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
460	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
461	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
462	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
463	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
464	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
465	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
466	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
467	\end{corollary}
468	
469	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
470	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
471	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
472	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
473	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
474	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
475	\begin{proof}
476	  
477	  
478	  
479	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
480	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
481	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
482	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
483	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
484	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
485	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
486	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
487	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
488	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
489	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
490	
491	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
492	  Theorem~REF as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
493	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
494	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
495	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
496	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
497	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
498	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
499	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
500	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
501	\end{proof}
502	
503	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
504	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
505	
506	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
507	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
508	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
509	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
510	
511	\begin{enumerate}
512	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
513	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
514	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
515	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
516	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
517	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
518	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
519	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
520	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
521	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
522	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
523	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
524	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
525	\end{enumerate}
526	
527	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
528	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
529	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
530	Corollary~REF are formalized in
531	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
532	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.

798	
799	For a commutative ring `R`, an ideal `I ⊆ R`, and two `R`-modules `M, M'` with
800	an `R`-linear equivalence `e : M ≃ₗ[R] M'`, we have `depth I M = depth I M'`.
801	
802	This is the standard "depth is an invariant of the isomorphism class" fact;
803	the proof has two steps: (1) the side-condition `I • ⊤ = ⊤` is preserved
804	under linear equivalence, and (2) the regular-sequence supremum sets agree
805	via `LinearEquiv.isRegular_congr`. Both halves are direct Mathlib calls; the
806	helper is axiom-clean.
807	
808	iter-193 Lane G structural advance: this helper is the substrate piece
809	needed to identify `depth(M)` with `depth(R^k)` for `M` finite free, which
810	in turn closes the `pd(M) = 0` base case of the Auslander–Buchsbaum
811	formula modulo the `depth(R^k) = depth(R)` direct-sum step (a separate
812	substrate item; the file's residual sorries are narrowed to that one
813	named piece). -/
814	lemma depth_eq_of_linearEquiv {R : Type u} [CommRing R] (I : Ideal R)
815	    {M M' : Type v} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [AddCommGroup M'] [Module R M']
816	    (e : M ≃ₗ[R] M') :
817	    depth I M = depth I M' := by
818	  -- Step 1: `I • ⊤ = ⊤` is preserved under the linear equivalence.
819	  have hcond : (I • (⊤ : Submodule R M) = ⊤) ↔ (I • (⊤ : Submodule R M') = ⊤) := by
820	    have e_top : Submodule.map (e : M →ₗ[R] M') (⊤ : Submodule R M) = ⊤ := by
821	      rw [Submodule.map_top]; exact LinearEquiv.range e
822	    have e_symm_top :
823	        Submodule.map (e.symm : M' →ₗ[R] M) (⊤ : Submodule R M') = ⊤ := by
824	      rw [Submodule.map_top]; exact LinearEquiv.range e.symm
825	    refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
826	    · intro h
827	      have hmap :=
828	        Submodule.map_smul'' I (⊤ : Submodule R M) (e : M →ₗ[R] M')
829	      rw [h, e_top] at hmap
830	      exact hmap.symm
831	    · intro h
832	      have hmap :=
833	        Submodule.map_smul'' I (⊤ : Submodule R M') (e.symm : M' →ₗ[R] M)
834	      rw [h, e_symm_top] at hmap
835	      exact hmap.symm
836	  -- Step 2: the `sSup` sets agree via `LinearEquiv.isRegular_congr`.
837	  unfold depth
838	  by_cases h : I • (⊤ : Submodule R M) = ⊤
839	  · simp [if_pos h, if_pos (hcond.mp h)]
840	  · rw [if_neg h, if_neg (mt hcond.mpr h)]

846	    · rintro ⟨rs, hlen, hmem, hreg⟩

851	/-! ## §5. The Auslander–Buchsbaum formula
852	
853	For a nonzero finite module `M` of finite projective dimension over a
854	Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`, the **Auslander–Buchsbaum formula** reads
855	```
856	  pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)
857	```
858	(Stacks tag 090V). The proof inducts on `depth(M)`: the base case
859	`depth(M) = 0` uses a minimal finite free resolution of `M` and the
860	"what is exact" criterion (Stacks 00MF) plus iterated application of the
861	depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma (§4) to bound `depth(R)` against the
862	resolution length; the inductive step picks a common non-zero-divisor
863	`x ∈ 𝔪` on both `R` and `M`, applies the snake lemma to obtain a minimal
864	finite free resolution of `M/xM` over `R/xR` of the same length, and uses
865	the inductive hypothesis on `M/xM` over `R/xR`.
866	
867	Blueprint reference: `thm:auslander_buchsbaum` (Stacks tag 090V). -/
868	
869	/-- **The Auslander–Buchsbaum formula.** Let `(R, 𝔪)` be a Noetherian local
870	ring and let `M` be a nonzero finite `R`-module of finite projective
871	dimension. Then
872	```
873	  pd_R(M) + depth_R(M) = depth(R).
874	```
875	
876	The hypothesis "finite projective dimension" is encoded by an explicit
877	upper bound `n : ℕ` on the projective dimension (so the formula compares
878	finite numeric quantities cleanly without `WithBot ℕ∞`-arithmetic
879	subtleties).
880	
881	iter-176+: the body is the induction on `depth(M)` outlined in the
882	chapter — base case via the minimal-finite-free-resolution +
883	"what is exact" + iterated `Module.depth_of_short_exact`; inductive step
884	via the snake lemma on multiplication by a common non-zero-divisor and
885	the inductive hypothesis on `M/xM` over `R/xR`. For the iter-175
886	file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
887	theorem auslander_buchsbaum_formula
888	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
889	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M]
890	    [Nontrivial M]
891	    (n : ℕ)
892	    (_hpd : _root_.Module.projectiveDimension R M = (n : WithBot ℕ∞)) :
893	    (n : ℕ∞) + Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M
894	      = Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R := by
895	  -- iter-193 Lane G: structural scaffold for Stacks 090V. We split on `n` to
896	  -- isolate the base case `pd_R(M) = 0` (where `M` is finite free over a

980	/-! ## §6. Cohen–Macaulay local rings
981	
982	A Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` is **Cohen–Macaulay** if its depth equals
983	its Krull dimension (Stacks tag 00N4). Mathlib `b80f227` has neither the
984	predicate nor the class — this file is the upstream gap-fill.
985	
986	Blueprint reference: `def:cohen_macaulay_local` (Stacks tag 00N4). -/
987	
988	/-- A Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` is **Cohen–Macaulay** if its depth
989	equals its Krull dimension: `depth(R) = dim R`.
990	
991	Encoded as a `Prop`-valued type class so downstream consumers can write
992	`[CohenMacaulay R]` and use Cohen–Macaulay as a hypothesis. Mathlib at the
993	pinned commit (`b80f227`) does not expose any Cohen–Macaulay predicate;
994	this is the upstream gap-fill.
995	
996	iter-176+: the predicate is `Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R =
997	ringKrullDim R`. For the iter-175 file-skeleton the carrier definition is a
998	typed `sorry` at the `Prop` level — substantively, the predicate is the
999	named equality, but we package it as a `class` so use sites are uniform. -/
1000	class CohenMacaulay (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
1001	    [IsNoetherianRing R] : Prop where
1002	  /-- The Cohen–Macaulay equation: `depth(R) = ringKrullDim R`. The numeric
1003	  comparison is in `WithBot ℕ∞` after coercion of the `ℕ∞`-valued depth. -/
1004	  depth_eq_krullDim :
1005	    (Module.depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R : WithBot ℕ∞) = ringKrullDim R
1006	
1007	/-! ## §7. Regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay
1008	
1009	The consumer-facing input for A.4.a: every regular Noetherian local ring is
1010	Cohen–Macaulay (Stacks tag 00OD). The direct proof: pick a minimal
1011	generating set `x_1, …, x_d` of `𝔪` (where `d = dim R`), use that `R` is a
1012	domain (Stacks 00NQ) to start an `R`-regular sequence, and induct on
1013	dimension — each `R/(x_1, …, x_c)` is again regular of dimension `d - c`,
1014	so `x_1, …, x_d` is an `R`-regular sequence and `depth(R) ≥ d`. The reverse
1015	inequality `depth(R) ≤ dim R` is the standard depth bound (Stacks 00LK).
1016	
1017	Blueprint reference: `cor:regular_cohen_macaulay` (Stacks tag 00OD). -/
1018	
1019	namespace CohenMacaulay
1020	
1021	/-! ### Helper 1 (axiom-clean): length-bound on regular sequences
1022	
1023	For a Noetherian local ring `R`, every `R`-regular sequence has length at most
1024	`ringKrullDim R`. This is the **upper bound** half of Stacks 00OD: it is the
1025	specialisation of the equality
1026	`ringKrullDim (R / ofList rs) + rs.length = ringKrullDim R`
1027	(`ringKrullDim_add_length_eq_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular`) to the observation that
1028	`ringKrullDim (R / ofList rs) ≥ 0` whenever the quotient is nontrivial, which it
1029	is precisely because `IsRegular` rules out `rs • ⊤ = ⊤`.
1030	
1031	iter-181 Lane G: closed kernel-clean. -/
1032	private lemma length_le_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular
1033	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
1034	    {rs : List R} (h : RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs) :
1035	    (rs.length : WithBot ℕ∞) ≤ ringKrullDim R := by
1036	  have heq := ringKrullDim_add_length_eq_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular rs h
1037	  have hntq : Nontrivial (R ⧸ Ideal.ofList rs) := by
1038	    rw [Ideal.Quotient.nontrivial_iff]
1039	    intro habs
1040	    apply h.top_ne_smul
1041	    change (⊤ : Submodule R R) = (Ideal.ofList rs) • ⊤
1042	    rw [habs]; simp
1043	  have hnn : (0 : WithBot ℕ∞) ≤ ringKrullDim (R ⧸ Ideal.ofList rs) :=
1044	    ringKrullDim_nonneg_of_nontrivial
1045	  calc (rs.length : WithBot ℕ∞)
1046	      = 0 + (rs.length : WithBot ℕ∞) := by simp
1047	    _ ≤ ringKrullDim (R ⧸ Ideal.ofList rs) + (rs.length : WithBot ℕ∞) := by gcongr
1048	    _ = ringKrullDim R := heq

1068	
1069	**Statement.** For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` and `x ∈ 𝔪 \ 𝔪²`, the
1070	cotangent space of `R / (x)` has dimension one less than that of `R`:
1071	```
1072	finrank κ' (CotangentSpace (R/(x))) + 1 = finrank κ (CotangentSpace R)
1073	```
1074	where `κ = R / 𝔪` and `κ' = (R/(x)) / 𝔪'` are the two residue fields
1075	(canonically isomorphic via the natural quotient `R/𝔪 ≃ (R/(x))/𝔪'`).
1076

1078	`exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` below: once Stacks 00NQ
1079	(regular local ⟹ domain) is filled in, this dim-drop is what upgrades
1080	`R/(x)` of dimension `k` back to `IsRegularLocalRing` via
1081	`IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace`.

1083	**Proof structure** (Bourbaki-style via `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank`):
1084	1. The R-algebra map `π : R → R/(x)` induces a κ-linear surjection of
1085	   cotangent spaces `f : 𝔪.Cotangent → 𝔪'.Cotangent` via
1086	   `Ideal.mapCotangent_surjective_of_comap_eq` (which applies because
1087	   `(maximalIdeal R).comap π = ker π ⊔ maximalIdeal R = (x) ⊔ 𝔪 = 𝔪`).
1088	2. The kernel of `f` is the R-submodule generated by the image `x̄` of `x`
1089	   in `𝔪.Cotangent` (via `Ideal.mapCotangent_ker_of_surjective` — the
1090	   kernel is `(Submodule.comap 𝔪.subtype ((x) ⊓ 𝔪)).map 𝔪.toCotangent`

1095	   `finrank κ (Submodule.span κ {x̄}) = 1` via `finrank_span_singleton`.

1218	      -- in the RHS uses v₀ directly, not the explicit expr).

1222	        IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := by
1223	      apply le_antisymm
1224	      · rw [Submodule.span_le]
1225	        rintro y hy
1226	        rcases hy with rfl | hy
1227	        · exact hx
1228	        · have hy_V : y ∈ V := Finset.mem_of_mem_erase (by exact_mod_cast hy)
1229	          rw [← hV_span]
1230	          exact Submodule.subset_span (by exact_mod_cast hy_V)

1232	        intro v hv
1233	        by_cases hv_eq : v = v₀
1234	        · rw [hv_eq]; exact hv₀_in_new
1235	        · refine Submodule.subset_span ?_
1236	          right
1237	          exact_mod_cast Finset.mem_erase.mpr ⟨hv_eq, by exact_mod_cast hv⟩
1238	    -- Step 6: 𝔪 R' = Ideal.span (mkx '' (V.erase v₀)).
1239	    -- First, 𝔪 R' = Ideal.map mkx 𝔪 R, and mkx x = 0.
1240	    set mkx : R →+* (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}) := Ideal.Quotient.mk _ with hmkx_def
1241	    have h_mkx_x : mkx x = 0 :=
1242	      Ideal.Quotient.eq_zero_iff_mem.mpr (Submodule.mem_span_singleton_self x)

1244	        (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x})) =
1245	        IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := by

1247	          (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}))).IsMaximal :=
1248	        Ideal.comap_isMaximal_of_surjective _ Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective
1249	      exact (IsLocalRing.isMaximal_iff R).mp hmax

1251	        Ideal.map mkx (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) := by
1252	      conv_rhs => rw [← hcomap_eq]
1253	      exact (Ideal.map_comap_of_surjective _ Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective _).symm

1272	          · exact Submodule.zero_mem _
1273	          · exact Submodule.subset_span hy
1274	        · exact Ideal.span_mono (Set.subset_insert _ _)

1290	  · -- (≥): (𝔪 R).spanFinrank ≤ (𝔪 (R/(x))).spanFinrank + 1.
1291	    -- Lift-and-cons strategy: a min gen set T of 𝔪 (R/(x)) lifts to T_lift ⊆
1292	    -- 𝔪 R via `Function.surjInv` of `Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective`; the union
1293	    -- `T_lift ∪ {x}` generates 𝔪 R since 𝔪 R = (Ideal.span {x}) ⊔ (lift of 𝔪').
1294	    classical
1295	    set R' : Type u := R ⧸ Ideal.span {x} with hR'_def
1296	    let mkx : R →+* R' := Ideal.Quotient.mk _
1297	    let g : R' → R := Function.surjInv Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective

1299	    -- Get min gen finset of 𝔪'.
1300	    have h𝔪'_fg : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R').FG := Ideal.fg_of_isNoetherianRing _
1301	    obtain ⟨T, hT_card, hT_span⟩ :=
1302	      Submodule.FG.exists_span_finset_card_eq_spanFinrank h𝔪'_fg
1303	    -- T : Finset R', T.card = spanFinrank 𝔪', span R' T = 𝔪'.
1304	    let T_lift : Finset R := T.image g
1305	    let U : Finset R := insert x T_lift
1306	    -- Step A: U generates 𝔪 R via the comap identification.
1307	    -- Comap mkx (𝔪 R') = 𝔪 R, since R is local and mkx is surjective.
1308	    have hcomap_eq : Ideal.comap mkx (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R') =
1309	        IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := by
1310	      have hmax : (Ideal.comap mkx (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R')).IsMaximal :=
1311	        Ideal.comap_isMaximal_of_surjective _ Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective
1312	      exact (IsLocalRing.isMaximal_iff R).mp hmax
1313	    have hker_eq : RingHom.ker mkx = Ideal.span {x} := Ideal.mk_ker
1314	    -- Step B: 𝔪 R = (Ideal.span T_lift) ⊔ (Ideal.span {x}).
1315	    -- Compute: comap mkx (map mkx (Ideal.span T_lift)) = Ideal.span T_lift ⊔ ker mkx.
1316	    have hmap_T_lift : Ideal.map mkx (Ideal.span (T_lift : Set R)) =
1317	        IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R' := by
1318	      rw [Ideal.map_span]
1319	      -- mkx '' T_lift = T (as sets).
1320	      have him : mkx '' (T_lift : Set R) = (T : Set R') := by
1321	        ext y
1322	        simp only [Set.mem_image, Finset.coe_image, T_lift]
1323	        refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
1324	        · rintro ⟨a, ⟨b, hbT, rfl⟩, rfl⟩
1325	          rw [hg b]; exact hbT
1326	        · intro hyT
1327	          exact ⟨g y, ⟨y, hyT, rfl⟩, hg y⟩
1328	      rw [him]; exact hT_span
1329	    have h𝔪R_decomp : IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R =
1330	        Ideal.span (T_lift : Set R) ⊔ Ideal.span {x} := by
1331	      calc IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R
1332	          = Ideal.comap mkx (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R') := hcomap_eq.symm
1333	        _ = Ideal.comap mkx (Ideal.map mkx (Ideal.span (T_lift : Set R))) := by
1334	              rw [hmap_T_lift]
1335	        _ = Ideal.span (T_lift : Set R) ⊔ RingHom.ker mkx := by
1336	              rw [Ideal.comap_map_of_surjective' mkx Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective]
1337	        _ = Ideal.span (T_lift : Set R) ⊔ Ideal.span {x} := by rw [hker_eq]

1344	      rw [hUeq, Submodule.span_union]

1350	          Submodule.spanFinrank_span_le_ncard_of_finite U.finite_toSet

1352	      _ ≤ T_lift.card + 1 := by
1353	          have := Finset.card_insert_le x T_lift
1354	          simpa [U] using this
1355	      _ ≤ T.card + 1 := by
1356	          have hle : T_lift.card ≤ T.card := Finset.card_image_le

1358	      _ = Submodule.spanFinrank (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R') + 1 := by

1361	/-! ### Helper 2 (substantive typed `sorry`): system-of-parameters as a regular sequence
1362	
1363	For a regular local ring `R` of Krull dimension `d = (maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank`,
1364	a minimal generating set `x_1, …, x_d` of the maximal ideal `𝔪` is an `R`-regular
1365	sequence. This is the **lower bound** half of Stacks 00OD.
1366	
1367	The proof in Stacks 00OD uses:
1368	1. A regular local ring is an integral domain (Stacks 00NQ).
1369	2. For each `c < d`, the quotient `R / (x_1, …, x_c)` is again a regular local
1370	   ring, of Krull dimension `d - c` (Krull's principal ideal theorem +
1371	   `lemma-one-equation`).
1372	
1373	Step (1) — `IsRegularLocalRing R ⟹ IsDomain R` — is **not present in Mathlib at
1374	the pinned commit** (`b80f227`); a `lean_leansearch` for "regular local ring is
1375	a domain" returns nothing relevant. Step (2) requires the regular-quotient
1376	inductive structure (Stacks 00NQ + height-one quotient regularity), which is
1377	similarly absent. Both are substantive multi-iter content.
1378	
1379	The signature here is the non-tautological existence statement: a list `rs : List R`
1380	with `rs.length = (maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank`, `rs ⊆ maximalIdeal R`, and
1381	`IsRegular R rs`. Downstream, `of_regular` consumes this directly to close the
1382	`depth ≥ d` lower bound.
1383	
1384	iter-185 Lane G **STRUCTURAL SCAFFOLD**: the main lemma is structurally
1385	complete by strong induction on `n = spanFinrank R`. The inductive step is
1386	factored into `regularLocal_inductive_step` (axiom-clean: lift-and-cons
1387	assembly via Mathlib's `IsRegular.cons'`) plus the Mathlib-gap helper
1388	`exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` (typed `sorry`,
1389	Stacks 00NQ + 00NU consolidated). The substrate work (`IsRegularLocalRing ⟹
1390	IsDomain` + Krull's PIT for quotient-by-minimal-generator) is genuinely
1391	multi-iter; the iter-185 progress is the structural extraction of the
1392	Mathlib gap into a narrow, well-typed helper, leaving the assembly
1393	axiom-clean for the gap-fill iteration to finish in `~5 LOC` once the
1394	helper closes. -/
1395	
1396	/-! ### Narrow substrate gap (Stacks 00NQ): regular local ring is a domain
1397	
1398	The Auslander–Buchsbaum-route closure of "regular local ring is Cohen–Macaulay"
1399	ultimately reduces to **Stacks 00NQ**: every regular local Noetherian ring
1400	is an integral domain. Mathlib at the pinned commit `b80f227` does not expose
1401	this implication (`lean_leansearch` for "regular local ring is a domain"
1402	returns nothing relevant; `lean_loogle` for `IsRegularLocalRing _ → IsDomain _`
1403	returns empty).
1404	
1405	Stacks 00NQ proof sketch (induction on `dim R = spanFinrank 𝔪`):
1406	* Base `dim R = 0`: then `𝔪 = ⊥`, hence `R` is a field, hence a domain.
1407	* Step `dim R = d + 1 ≥ 1`:
1408	  - Pick `x ∈ 𝔪 \ 𝔪²` (axiom-clean via Nakayama; see
1409	    `exists_notMemSq_of_spanFinrank_pos` below).
1410	  - By the cotangent dim-drop (`finrank_cotangentSpace_quot_span_singleton_succ`,
1411	    iter-188 Lane G1 closure) **plus** Krull's principal ideal theorem
1412	    (`ringKrullDim_quotient_span_singleton_succ_eq_ringKrullDim_of_mem_jacobson`
1413	    gives the `≥` half), `R / (x)` is regular local of dim `d`.
1414	  - By induction, `R / (x)` is a domain, hence `(x)` is a prime ideal of `R`.
1415	  - Let `𝔭` be a minimal prime of `R` with `𝔭 ⊆ (x)`. For `y ∈ 𝔭`, write
1416	    `y = xz`; since `𝔭` is prime and `x ∉ 𝔭` (else `x ∈ 𝔭 ⊆ (x)` doesn't
1417	    immediately give a contradiction but combined with `dim R/(x) = d` and
1418	    `x ∈ 𝔭` height-zero we get `𝔭 = 0` which is the conclusion), we get
1419	    `z ∈ 𝔭`. Hence `𝔭 ⊆ x 𝔭`, so iterating, `𝔭 ⊆ ∩ x^n 𝔭 ⊆ ∩ 𝔪^n = 0`
1420	    (Krull intersection). So `𝔭 = 0`, and `R` is a domain.
1421	
1422	Full formalization is ~300 LOC (involves Krull's PIT, prime avoidance,
1423	Krull intersection, minimal-primes-are-finite); deferred to a future iter
1424	or Mathlib upstream contribution. -/
1425	
1426	/-- **Axiom-clean Nakayama witness.** For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`
1427	with `spanFinrank 𝔪 ≥ 1`, there exists `x ∈ 𝔪` with `x ∉ 𝔪²`.
1428	
1429	This is the "cotangent space is nonzero" content: by Nakayama, if `𝔪 ⊆ 𝔪²`
1430	then `𝔪 = 0` (so `spanFinrank 𝔪 = 0`), contradicting the hypothesis. -/
1431	private lemma exists_notMemSq_of_spanFinrank_pos
1432	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
1433	    (h : 0 < (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank) :
1434	    ∃ x ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R, x ∉ (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) ^ 2 := by
1435	  -- By contradiction: assume 𝔪 ⊆ 𝔪², then by Nakayama 𝔪 = 0, but spanFinrank 𝔪 ≥ 1.
1436	  by_contra h_neg
1437	  push Not at h_neg
1438	  have h𝔪_le_sq : IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R ≤ (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) ^ 2 := h_neg
1439	  -- 𝔪² = 𝔪 • 𝔪.
1440	  have hsq : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R : Submodule R R) ^ 2
1441	      = (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) • (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R : Submodule R R) := by
1442	    rw [sq, ← Ideal.smul_eq_mul]
1443	  have h𝔪_fg : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R : Submodule R R).FG :=
1444	    Ideal.fg_of_isNoetherianRing _
1445	  -- Use Submodule.le_of_le_smul_of_le_jacobson_bot with N = ⊥.
1446	  have hjac : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) ≤ (⊥ : Ideal R).jacobson :=
1447	    IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal_le_jacobson _
1448	  have h_le_smul : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R : Submodule R R)
1449	      ≤ ⊥ ⊔ (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) •
1450	        (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R : Submodule R R) := by
1451	    rw [bot_sup_eq, ← hsq]; exact h𝔪_le_sq
1452	  have h𝔪_bot : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R : Submodule R R) ≤ ⊥ :=
1453	    Submodule.le_of_le_smul_of_le_jacobson_bot h𝔪_fg hjac h_le_smul
1454	  have h𝔪_eq_bot : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) = ⊥ := le_bot_iff.mp h𝔪_bot
1455	  have h_span : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = 0 := by
1456	    rw [h𝔪_eq_bot]; exact Submodule.spanFinrank_bot
1457	  omega

1459	/-! ### Helper for Stacks 00NQ base case (iter-190 Lane G, axiom-clean).
1460	
1461	For a Noetherian local ring `R` with `(maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = 0`, the
1462	ring `R` is a field, hence a domain. The maximal ideal collapses to `⊥` via
1463	`Submodule.spanFinrank_eq_zero_iff_eq_bot` (under FG), and

1466	`IsField.isDomain` gives `IsDomain R`. -/
1467	private lemma isDomain_of_isLocalRing_of_spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_eq_zero
1468	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
1469	    (h : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = 0) : IsDomain R := by
1470	  have h𝔪_fg : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).FG :=
1471	    Ideal.fg_of_isNoetherianRing _
1472	  have h𝔪_bot : IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R = ⊥ :=
1473	    (Submodule.spanFinrank_eq_zero_iff_eq_bot h𝔪_fg).mp h
1474	  have hField : IsField R :=
1475	    IsLocalRing.isField_iff_maximalIdeal_eq.mpr h𝔪_bot
1476	  exact hField.isDomain

1584	/-! ### Helper iter-191 Lane G (axiom-clean): zero-divisor witness from a minimal prime
1585	
1586	For a commutative ring `R` and a minimal prime `𝔭 ∈ minimalPrimes R`, every
1587	element of `𝔭` is a zero-divisor in `R`. Concretely: for any `x ∈ 𝔭`, there
1588	exists `y ∈ R, y ≠ 0` with `x * y = 0`.
1589	
1590	Proof: minimal primes are disjoint from non-zero-divisors via
1591	`Ideal.disjoint_nonZeroDivisors_of_mem_minimalPrimes`, so `x ∈ 𝔭` ⟹
1592	`x ∉ nonZeroDivisors R` ⟹ `∃ y ≠ 0, x * y = 0`.
1593	
1594	This packages the Stacks-00NQ-relevant first step for the `x ∈ 𝔭` case of
1595	`isDomain_of_regularLocal`: when `(x)` is a minimal prime, `x` is a zero-divisor,
1596	so the obstruction reduces to derivation of a contradiction from the
1597	zero-divisor witness (using the regular-local structure of `R` and `R/(x)`). -/
1598	private lemma exists_ne_zero_mul_eq_zero_of_mem_minimalPrimes
1599	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] {𝔭 : Ideal R} (h𝔭 : 𝔭 ∈ minimalPrimes R)
1600	    {x : R} (hx : x ∈ 𝔭) :
1601	    ∃ y : R, y ≠ 0 ∧ x * y = 0 := by
1602	  have hdisj : Disjoint (𝔭 : Set R) (nonZeroDivisors R : Set R) :=
1603	    Ideal.disjoint_nonZeroDivisors_of_mem_minimalPrimes h𝔭
1604	  have hxNot : x ∉ nonZeroDivisors R := fun hxNZD =>
1605	    (Set.disjoint_left.mp hdisj) hx hxNZD
1606	  have hExistsZD : ¬ ∀ z, x * z = 0 → z = 0 := fun h => hxNot <| by
1607	    rw [mem_nonZeroDivisors_iff]
1608	    refine ⟨h, fun z hz => h z (by rw [mul_comm]; exact hz)⟩
1609	  push Not at hExistsZD
1610	  obtain ⟨y, hxy, hy⟩ := hExistsZD
1611	  exact ⟨y, hy, hxy⟩

1863	    exact haux _ R rfl

1879	      ih (R ⧸ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) hdim_quot

1885	      (I := (⊥ : Ideal R)) (J := Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) bot_le

2068	/-- **Lane G inductive step (iter-185 structural scaffold).** Packages the

2070	`k + 1`, plus the inductive hypothesis at dimension `k` (universally
2071	quantified in the ring), produce a regular sequence of length `k + 1` in
2072	the maximal ideal of `R`.

2074	The body is **axiom-clean** modulo the typed-`sorry` substrate helper
2075	`exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ`. Assembly path:
2076	
2077	1. Helper extracts `x ∈ 𝔪` with `IsSMulRegular R x` AND `IsRegularLocalRing
2078	   (R⧸(x))` of `spanFinrank = k`.
2079	2. IH applied on `R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}` produces a regular sequence
2080	   `rs'_q : List (R ⧸ (x))` of length `k` in the maximal ideal there.
2081	3. Lift `rs'_q` to `rs : List R` via `Function.surjInv` of
2082	   `Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective`; the section property gives
2083	   `rs.map (Ideal.Quotient.mk _) = rs'_q`.
2084	4. Members of `rs` lie in `𝔪` because the maximal ideal of `R⧸(x)` is the
2085	   image of `𝔪` (`IsLocalRing.le_maximalIdeal` applied to the comap chain).
2086	5. Cons via `RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular.cons'` to form the length-`(k+1)`
2087	   sequence `x :: rs`.

2089	This factoring isolates the Mathlib gap to one narrow typed `sorry` instead of
2090	spreading it across the lemma body; future iterations can attack Stacks 00NQ
2091	+ 00NU directly. -/
2092	private lemma regularLocal_inductive_step {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
2093	    [IsNoetherianRing R] [IsRegularLocalRing R] {k : ℕ}
2094	    (hdim : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = k + 1)
2095	    (IH : ∀ (R' : Type u) [CommRing R'] [IsLocalRing R'] [IsNoetherianRing R']
2096	            [IsRegularLocalRing R'],
2097	            (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R').spanFinrank = k →
2098	            ∃ rs : List R', rs.length = k ∧
2099	              (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R') ∧
2100	              RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R' rs) :
2101	    ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = k + 1 ∧
2102	      (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) ∧
2103	      RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs := by
2104	  -- Step 1: extract `x ∈ 𝔪` regular on `R` with `R ⧸ (x)` regular local of
2105	  -- `spanFinrank = k`.  This is the consolidated Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate.
2106	  obtain ⟨x, hxMem, hxReg, hRLR, hdim_quot⟩ :=
2107	    exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ hdim
2108	  -- Step 2: apply IH on `R ⧸ (x)` — this gives a regular sequence of length
2109	  -- `k` on the quotient, valued in its maximal ideal.
2110	  obtain ⟨rs'_q, hlen_q, hmem_q, hreg_q⟩ := IH (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}) hdim_quot
2111	  -- Step 3: lift `rs'_q : List (R ⧸ (x))` to `rs' : List R` via the right
2112	  -- inverse of the (surjective) quotient ring hom.
2113	  let mkq : R →+* R ⧸ Ideal.span {x} := Ideal.Quotient.mk _
2114	  let g : R ⧸ Ideal.span {x} → R := Function.surjInv Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective

2118	  have hmkmap : rs'.map mkq = rs'_q := by
2119	    change (rs'_q.map g).map mkq = rs'_q
2120	    rw [List.map_map]
2121	    conv_rhs => rw [← List.map_id rs'_q]

2134	        (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}))).IsMaximal :=
2135	      Ideal.comap_isMaximal_of_surjective _ Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective

2139	      (IsLocalRing.isMaximal_iff R).mp hmax_comap

2164	    --   * `R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}` — quotient by `Ideal.span {x}` as a Submodule R R;
2165	    --   * `QuotSMulTop x R = R ⧸ (x • ⊤)` — quotient by `x • ⊤` as a Submodule R R.

101	lemma top_eq_ofList_cons_smul_iff :
102	    (⊤ : Submodule R M) = Ideal.ofList (r :: rs) • ⊤ ↔
103	      (⊤ : Submodule R (QuotSMulTop r M)) = Ideal.ofList rs • ⊤ := by

109	namespace RingTheory.Sequence
110	
111	open scoped TensorProduct List
112	open Function Submodule QuotSMulTop
113	
114	variable (S M)
115	
116	section Definitions
117	
118	/-
119	In theory, regularity of `rs : List α` on `M` makes sense as soon as
120	`[Monoid α]`, `[AddCommGroup M]`, and `[DistribMulAction α M]`.
121	Instead of `Ideal.ofList (rs.take i) • (⊤ : Submodule R M)` we use
122	`⨆ (j : Fin i), rs[j] • (⊤ : AddSubgroup M)`.
123	However it's not clear that this is a useful generalization.
124	If we add the assumption `[SMulCommClass α α M]` this is essentially the same
125	as focusing on the commutative ring case, by passing to the monoid ring
126	`ℤ[abelianization of α]`.
127	-/
128	variable [CommRing R] [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M]
129	
130	open Ideal
131	
132	/-- A sequence `[r₁, …, rₙ]` is weakly regular on `M` iff `rᵢ` is regular on
133	`M⧸(r₁, …, rᵢ₋₁)M` for all `1 ≤ i ≤ n`. -/
134	@[mk_iff]
135	structure IsWeaklyRegular (rs : List R) : Prop where
136	  regular_mod_prev : ∀ i (h : i < rs.length),
137	    IsSMulRegular (M ⧸ (ofList (rs.take i) • ⊤ : Submodule R M)) rs[i]
138	
139	lemma isWeaklyRegular_iff_Fin (rs : List R) :
140	    IsWeaklyRegular M rs ↔ ∀ (i : Fin rs.length),
141	      IsSMulRegular (M ⧸ (ofList (rs.take i) • ⊤ : Submodule R M)) rs[i] :=
142	  Iff.trans (isWeaklyRegular_iff M rs) (Iff.symm Fin.forall_iff)
143	
144	/-- A weakly regular sequence `rs` on `M` is regular if also `M/rsM ≠ 0`. -/
145	@[mk_iff]
146	structure IsRegular (rs : List R) : Prop extends IsWeaklyRegular M rs where
147	  top_ne_smul : (⊤ : Submodule R M) ≠ Ideal.ofList rs • ⊤
148	
149	end Definitions
150	
151	section Congr
152	
153	variable {S M} [CommRing R] [CommRing S] [AddCommGroup M] [AddCommGroup M₂]
154	    [Module R M] [Module S M₂]
155	    {σ : R →+* S} {σ' : S →+* R} [RingHomInvPair σ σ'] [RingHomInvPair σ' σ]
156	
157	open DistribMulAction AddSubgroup in
158	private lemma _root_.AddHom.map_smul_top_toAddSubgroup_of_surjective
159	    {f : M →+ M₂} {as : List R} {bs : List S} (hf : Function.Surjective f)
160	    (h : List.Forall₂ (fun r s => ∀ x, f (r • x) = s • f x) as bs) :
161	    (Ideal.ofList as • ⊤ : Submodule R M).toAddSubgroup.map f =
162	      (Ideal.ofList bs • ⊤ : Submodule S M₂).toAddSubgroup := by
163	  induction h with
164	  | nil =>
165	    convert AddSubgroup.map_bot f using 1 <;>
166	      rw [Ideal.ofList_nil, bot_smul, bot_toAddSubgroup]
167	  | @cons r s _ _ h _ ih =>
168	    conv => congr <;> rw [Ideal.ofList_cons, sup_smul, sup_toAddSubgroup,
169	      ideal_span_singleton_smul, pointwise_smul_toAddSubgroup,
170	      top_toAddSubgroup, AddSubgroup.pointwise_smul_def]
171	    apply DFunLike.ext (f.comp (toAddMonoidEnd R M r))
172	      ((toAddMonoidEnd S M₂ s).comp f) at h
173	    rw [AddSubgroup.map_sup, ih, map_map, h, ← map_map,
174	      map_top_of_surjective f hf]
175	
176	lemma _root_.AddEquiv.isWeaklyRegular_congr {e : M ≃+ M₂} {as bs}
177	    (h : List.Forall₂ (fun (r : R) (s : S) => ∀ x, e (r • x) = s • e x) as bs) :
178	    IsWeaklyRegular M as ↔ IsWeaklyRegular M₂ bs := by
179	  conv => congr <;> rw [isWeaklyRegular_iff_Fin]
180	  let e' i : (M ⧸ (Ideal.ofList (as.take i) • ⊤ : Submodule R M)) ≃+
181	      M₂ ⧸ (Ideal.ofList (bs.take i) • ⊤ : Submodule S M₂) :=
182	    QuotientAddGroup.congr _ _ e <|
183	      AddHom.map_smul_top_toAddSubgroup_of_surjective e.surjective <|
184	        List.forall₂_take i h
185	  refine (finCongr h.length_eq).forall_congr @fun _ => (e' _).isSMulRegular_congr ?_
186	  exact (mkQ_surjective _).forall.mpr fun _ => congrArg (mkQ _) (h.get _ _ _)
187	
188	lemma _root_.LinearEquiv.isWeaklyRegular_congr' (e : M ≃ₛₗ[σ] M₂) (rs : List R) :
189	    IsWeaklyRegular M rs ↔ IsWeaklyRegular M₂ (rs.map σ) :=
190	  e.toAddEquiv.isWeaklyRegular_congr <| List.forall₂_map_right_iff.mpr <|
191	    List.forall₂_same.mpr fun r _ x => e.map_smul' r x
192	
193	lemma _root_.LinearEquiv.isWeaklyRegular_congr [Module R M₂] (e : M ≃ₗ[R] M₂) (rs : List R) :
194	    IsWeaklyRegular M rs ↔ IsWeaklyRegular M₂ rs :=
195	  Iff.trans (e.isWeaklyRegular_congr' rs) <| iff_of_eq <| congrArg _ rs.map_id
196	
197	lemma _root_.AddEquiv.isRegular_congr {e : M ≃+ M₂} {as bs}
198	    (h : List.Forall₂ (fun (r : R) (s : S) => ∀ x, e (r • x) = s • e x) as bs) :
199	    IsRegular M as ↔ IsRegular M₂ bs := by
200	  conv => congr <;> rw [isRegular_iff, ne_comm, ← Quotient.nontrivial_iff]
201	  let e' := QuotientAddGroup.congr _ _ e <|
202	    AddHom.map_smul_top_toAddSubgroup_of_surjective e.surjective h
203	  exact and_congr (e.isWeaklyRegular_congr h) e'.nontrivial_congr
204	
205	lemma _root_.LinearEquiv.isRegular_congr' (e : M ≃ₛₗ[σ] M₂) (rs : List R) :
206	    IsRegular M rs ↔ IsRegular M₂ (rs.map σ) :=
207	  e.toAddEquiv.isRegular_congr <| List.forall₂_map_right_iff.mpr <|
208	    List.forall₂_same.mpr fun r _ x => e.map_smul' r x
209	
210	lemma _root_.LinearEquiv.isRegular_congr [Module R M₂] (e : M ≃ₗ[R] M₂) (rs : List R) :
211	    IsRegular M rs ↔ IsRegular M₂ rs :=
212	  Iff.trans (e.isRegular_congr' rs) <| iff_of_eq <| congrArg _ rs.map_id
213	
214	end Congr
215	
216	lemma isWeaklyRegular_map_algebraMap_iff [CommRing R] [CommRing S]
217	    [Algebra R S] [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [Module S M]
218	    [IsScalarTower R S M] (rs : List R) :
219	    IsWeaklyRegular M (rs.map (algebraMap R S)) ↔ IsWeaklyRegular M rs :=
220	  (AddEquiv.refl M).isWeaklyRegular_congr <| List.forall₂_map_left_iff.mpr <|
221	    List.forall₂_same.mpr fun r _ => algebraMap_smul S r
222	
223	variable [CommRing R] [AddCommGroup M] [AddCommGroup M₂] [AddCommGroup M₃]
224	    [AddCommGroup M₄] [Module R M] [Module R M₂] [Module R M₃] [Module R M₄]
225	
226	@[simp]
227	lemma isWeaklyRegular_cons_iff (r : R) (rs : List R) :
228	    IsWeaklyRegular M (r :: rs) ↔
229	      IsSMulRegular M r ∧ IsWeaklyRegular (QuotSMulTop r M) rs :=
230	  have := Eq.trans (congrArg (· • ⊤) Ideal.ofList_nil) (bot_smul ⊤)
231	  let e i := quotOfListConsSMulTopEquivQuotSMulTopInner M r (rs.take i)
232	  Iff.trans (isWeaklyRegular_iff_Fin _ _) <| Iff.trans Fin.forall_iff_succ <|
233	    and_congr ((quotEquivOfEqBot _ this).isSMulRegular_congr r) <|
234	      Iff.trans (forall_congr' fun i => (e i).isSMulRegular_congr (rs.get i))
235	        (isWeaklyRegular_iff_Fin _ _).symm
236	
237	lemma isWeaklyRegular_cons_iff' (r : R) (rs : List R) :
238	    IsWeaklyRegular M (r :: rs) ↔
239	      IsSMulRegular M r ∧
240	        IsWeaklyRegular (QuotSMulTop r M)
241	          (rs.map (Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {r}))) :=
242	  Iff.trans (isWeaklyRegular_cons_iff M r rs) <| and_congr_right' <|
243	    Iff.symm <| isWeaklyRegular_map_algebraMap_iff (R ⧸ Ideal.span {r}) _ rs
244	
245	@[simp]
246	lemma isRegular_cons_iff (r : R) (rs : List R) :
247	    IsRegular M (r :: rs) ↔
248	      IsSMulRegular M r ∧ IsRegular (QuotSMulTop r M) rs := by
249	  rw [isRegular_iff, isRegular_iff, isWeaklyRegular_cons_iff M r rs,
250	    ne_eq, top_eq_ofList_cons_smul_iff, and_assoc]
251	
252	lemma isRegular_cons_iff' (r : R) (rs : List R) :
253	    IsRegular M (r :: rs) ↔
254	      IsSMulRegular M r ∧ IsRegular (QuotSMulTop r M)
255	          (rs.map (Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {r}))) := by
256	  conv => congr <;> rw [isRegular_iff, ne_eq]
257	  rw [isWeaklyRegular_cons_iff', ← restrictScalars_inj R (R ⧸ _),
258	    ← Ideal.map_ofList, ← Ideal.Quotient.algebraMap_eq, Ideal.smul_restrictScalars,
259	    restrictScalars_top, top_eq_ofList_cons_smul_iff, and_assoc]
260	
261	variable {M}
262	
263	namespace IsWeaklyRegular
264	
265	variable (R M) in
266	@[simp] lemma nil : IsWeaklyRegular M ([] : List R) :=
267	  .mk (False.elim <| Nat.not_lt_zero · ·)
268	
269	lemma cons {r : R} {rs : List R} (h1 : IsSMulRegular M r)
270	    (h2 : IsWeaklyRegular (QuotSMulTop r M) rs) : IsWeaklyRegular M (r :: rs) :=
271	  (isWeaklyRegular_cons_iff M r rs).mpr ⟨h1, h2⟩
272	
273	lemma cons' {r : R} {rs : List R} (h1 : IsSMulRegular M r)
274	    (h2 : IsWeaklyRegular (QuotSMulTop r M)
275	            (rs.map (Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {r})))) :
276	    IsWeaklyRegular M (r :: rs) :=
277	  (isWeaklyRegular_cons_iff' M r rs).mpr ⟨h1, h2⟩
278	
279	/-- Weakly regular sequences can be inductively characterized by:
280	* The empty sequence is weakly regular on any module.
281	* If `r` is regular on `M` and `rs` is a weakly regular sequence on `M⧸rM` then
282	  the sequence obtained from `rs` by prepending `r` is weakly regular on `M`.
283	
284	This is the induction principle produced by the inductive definition above.
285	The motive will usually be valued in `Prop`, but `Sort*` works too. -/
286	@[induction_eliminator]
287	def recIterModByRegular
288	    {motive : (M : Type v) → [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → (rs : List R) →
289	      IsWeaklyRegular M rs → Sort*}
290	    (nil : (M : Type v) → [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → motive M [] (nil R M))
291	    (cons : {M : Type v} → [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → (r : R) →
292	      (rs : List R) → (h1 : IsSMulRegular M r) →
293	      (h2 : IsWeaklyRegular (QuotSMulTop r M) rs) →
294	      (ih : motive (QuotSMulTop r M) rs h2) → motive M (r :: rs) (cons h1 h2)) :
295	    {M : Type v} → [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → {rs : List R} →
296	    (h : IsWeaklyRegular M rs) → motive M rs h
297	  | M, _, _, [], _ => nil M
298	  | M, _, _, r :: rs, h =>
299	    let ⟨h1, h2⟩ := (isWeaklyRegular_cons_iff M r rs).mp h
300	    cons r rs h1 h2 (recIterModByRegular nil cons h2)
301	
302	/-- A simplified version of `IsWeaklyRegular.recIterModByRegular` where the
303	motive is not allowed to depend on the proof of `IsWeaklyRegular`. -/
304	def ndrecIterModByRegular
305	    {motive : (M : Type v) → [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → (rs : List R) → Sort*}
306	    (nil : (M : Type v) → [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → motive M [])
307	    (cons : {M : Type v} → [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → (r : R) →
308	      (rs : List R) → IsSMulRegular M r → IsWeaklyRegular (QuotSMulTop r M) rs →
309	      motive (QuotSMulTop r M) rs → motive M (r :: rs))
310	    {M} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] {rs} :
311	    IsWeaklyRegular M rs → motive M rs :=
312	  recIterModByRegular (motive := fun M _ _ rs _ => motive M rs) nil cons
313	
314	/-- An alternate induction principle from `IsWeaklyRegular.recIterModByRegular`
315	where we mod out by successive elements in both the module and the base ring.
316	This is useful for propagating certain properties of the initial `M`, e.g.
317	faithfulness or freeness, throughout the induction. -/
318	def recIterModByRegularWithRing
319	    {motive : (R : Type u) → [CommRing R] → (M : Type v) → [AddCommGroup M] →
320	      [Module R M] → (rs : List R) → IsWeaklyRegular M rs → Sort*}
321	    (nil : (R : Type u) → [CommRing R] → (M : Type v) → [AddCommGroup M] →
322	      [Module R M] → motive R M [] (nil R M))
323	    (cons : {R : Type u} → [CommRing R] → {M : Type v} → [AddCommGroup M] →
324	      [Module R M] → (r : R) → (rs : List R) → (h1 : IsSMulRegular M r) →
325	      (h2 : IsWeaklyRegular (QuotSMulTop r M)
326	              (rs.map (Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {r})))) →
327	      (ih : motive (R ⧸ Ideal.span {r}) (QuotSMulTop r M)
328	              (rs.map (Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {r}))) h2) →
329	            motive R M (r :: rs) (cons' h1 h2)) :
330	    {R : Type u} → [CommRing R] → {M : Type v} → [AddCommGroup M] →
331	    [Module R M] → {rs : List R} → (h : IsWeaklyRegular M rs) → motive R M rs h
332	  | R, _, M, _, _, [], _ => nil R M
333	  | _, _, M, _, _, r :: rs, h =>
334	    let ⟨h1, h2⟩ := (isWeaklyRegular_cons_iff' M r rs).mp h
335	    cons r rs h1 h2 (recIterModByRegularWithRing nil cons h2)
336	  termination_by _ _ _ _ _ rs => List.length rs
337	
338	/-- A simplified version of `IsWeaklyRegular.recIterModByRegularWithRing` where
339	the motive is not allowed to depend on the proof of `IsWeaklyRegular`. -/
340	def ndrecWithRing
341	    {motive : (R : Type u) → [CommRing R] → (M : Type v) →
342	      [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → (rs : List R) → Sort*}
343	    (nil : (R : Type u) → [CommRing R] → (M : Type v) →
344	      [AddCommGroup M] → [Module R M] → motive R M [])
345	    (cons : {R : Type u} → [CommRing R] → {M : Type v} → [AddCommGroup M] →
346	      [Module R M] → (r : R) → (rs : List R) → IsSMulRegular M r →
347	      IsWeaklyRegular (QuotSMulTop r M)
348	        (rs.map (Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {r}))) →
349	      motive (R ⧸ Ideal.span {r}) (QuotSMulTop r M)

6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.Algebra.Module.FinitePresentation
9	public import Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dual.Lemmas
10	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.AssociatedPrime.Finiteness
11	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.AssociatedPrime.Localization
12	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalRing.ResidueField.Ideal
13	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.IsSMulRegular
14	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Support
15	
16	/-!
17	
18	# Hom(N,M) is subsingleton iff there exists a smul regular element of M in ann(N)
19	
20	Let `M` and `N` be `R`-modules. In this section we prove that `Hom(N,M)` is subsingleton iff
21	there exist `r : R`, such that `IsSMulRegular M r` and `r ∈ ann(N)`.
22	This is the case if `Depth[I](M) = 0`.
23	
24	## Main statements
25	
26	* `IsSMulRegular.subsingleton_linearMap_iff` : for `R` module `N M`, `Hom(N, M) = 0`
27	  iff there is a `M`-regular in `Module.annihilator R N`.
28	
29	-/
30	
31	public section
32	
33	open IsLocalRing LinearMap Module
34	
35	namespace IsSMulRegular
36	
37	variable {R M N : Type*} [CommRing R] [AddCommGroup M] [AddCommGroup N] [Module R M] [Module R N]
38	
39	lemma linearMap_subsingleton_of_mem_annihilator {r : R} (reg : IsSMulRegular M r)
40	    (mem_ann : r ∈ Module.annihilator R N) : Subsingleton (N →ₗ[R] M) := by
41	  apply subsingleton_of_forall_eq 0 (fun f ↦ ext fun x ↦ ?_)
42	  have : r • (f x) = r • 0 := by
43	    rw [smul_zero, ← map_smul, Module.mem_annihilator.mp mem_ann x, map_zero]
44	  simpa using reg this
45	
46	lemma subsingleton_linearMap_iff [IsNoetherianRing R] [Module.Finite R M] [Module.Finite R N] :
47	    Subsingleton (N →ₗ[R] M) ↔ ∃ r ∈ Module.annihilator R N, IsSMulRegular M r := by
48	  refine ⟨fun hom0 ↦ ?_, fun ⟨r, mem_ann, reg⟩ ↦
49	    linearMap_subsingleton_of_mem_annihilator reg mem_ann⟩
50	  cases subsingleton_or_nontrivial M
51	  · exact ⟨0, ⟨Submodule.zero_mem (Module.annihilator R N), IsSMulRegular.zero⟩⟩
52	  · by_contra! h
53	    have hexist : ∃ p ∈ associatedPrimes R M, Module.annihilator R N ≤ p := by
54	      rcases associatedPrimes.nonempty R M with ⟨Ia, hIa⟩
55	      apply (Ideal.subset_union_prime_finite (associatedPrimes.finite R M) Ia Ia _).mp
56	      · rw [biUnion_associatedPrimes_eq_compl_regular R M]
57	        exact fun r hr ↦ h r hr
58	      · exact fun I hin _ _ ↦ IsAssociatedPrime.isPrime hin
59	    rcases hexist with ⟨p, pass, hp⟩
60	    let _ := pass.isPrime
61	    let p' : PrimeSpectrum R := ⟨p, pass.isPrime⟩
62	    have loc_ne_zero : p' ∈ Module.support R N := Module.mem_support_iff_of_finite.mpr hp
63	    rw [Module.mem_support_iff] at loc_ne_zero
64	    let Rₚ := Localization.AtPrime p
65	    let Nₚ := LocalizedModule.AtPrime p'.asIdeal N
66	    let Mₚ := LocalizedModule.AtPrime p'.asIdeal M
67	    let Nₚ' := Nₚ ⧸ (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (Localization.AtPrime p)) • (⊤ : Submodule Rₚ Nₚ)
68	    have ntr : Nontrivial Nₚ' :=
69	      Submodule.Quotient.nontrivial_iff.mpr <| .symm <|
70	        Submodule.top_ne_ideal_smul_of_le_jacobson_annihilator <|
71	          IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal_le_jacobson _
72	    let Mₚ' := Mₚ ⧸ (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (Localization.AtPrime p)) • (⊤ : Submodule Rₚ Mₚ)
73	    let _ : Module p.ResidueField Nₚ' :=
74	      Module.instQuotientIdealSubmoduleHSMulTop Nₚ (maximalIdeal (Localization.AtPrime p))
75	    have := isAssociatedPrime_iff.mp <| AssociatedPrimes.mem_iff.mp
76	      (associatedPrimes.mem_associatedPrimes_atPrime_of_mem_associatedPrimes pass)
77	    rcases this.2 with ⟨x, hx⟩
78	    have : Nontrivial (Module.Dual p.ResidueField Nₚ') := by simpa using ntr
79	    rcases exists_ne (α := Module.Dual p.ResidueField Nₚ') 0 with ⟨g, hg⟩
80	    let to_res' : Nₚ' →ₗ[Rₚ] p.ResidueField := {
81	      __ := g
82	      map_smul' r x := by
83	        simp only [AddHom.toFun_eq_coe, coe_toAddHom, RingHom.id_apply]
84	        convert g.map_smul (Ideal.Quotient.mk _ r) x }
85	    let to_res : Nₚ →ₗ[Rₚ] p.ResidueField :=
86	      to_res'.comp ((maximalIdeal (Localization.AtPrime p)) • (⊤ : Submodule Rₚ Nₚ)).mkQ
87	    replace hx : maximalIdeal (Localization.AtPrime p) = (toSpanSingleton _ _ x).ker :=
88	      hx.trans (by simp [SetLike.ext_iff])
89	    let i : p.ResidueField →ₗ[Rₚ] Mₚ :=
90	      Submodule.liftQ _ (LinearMap.toSpanSingleton Rₚ Mₚ x) (le_of_eq hx)
91	    have inj1 : Function.Injective i :=
92	      LinearMap.ker_eq_bot.mp (Submodule.ker_liftQ_eq_bot _ _ _ (le_of_eq hx.symm))
93	    let f := i.comp to_res
94	    have f_ne0 : f ≠ 0 := by
95	      intro eq0
96	      absurd hg
97	      apply LinearMap.ext
98	      intro np'
99	      induction np' using Submodule.Quotient.induction_on with | _ np
100	      change to_res np = 0
101	      apply inj1
102	      change f np = _
103	      simp [eq0]
104	    absurd hom0
105	    let _ := Module.finitePresentation_of_finite R N
106	    contrapose f_ne0
107	    exact (Module.FinitePresentation.linearEquivMapExtendScalars
108	      p'.asIdeal.primeCompl).symm.map_eq_zero_iff.mp (Subsingleton.eq_zero _)
109	
110	end IsSMulRegular

6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.DFinsupp
9	public import Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.Quotient
10	public import Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.RightExactness
11	
12	/-!
13	# Reducing a module modulo an element of the ring
14	
15	Given a commutative ring `R` and an element `r : R`, the association
16	`M ↦ M ⧸ rM` extends to a functor on the category of `R`-modules. This functor
17	is isomorphic to the functor of tensoring by `R ⧸ (r)` on either side, but can
18	be more convenient to work with since we can work with quotient types instead
19	of fiddling with simple tensors.
20	
21	## Tags
22	
23	module, commutative algebra
24	-/
25	
26	@[expose] public section
27	
28	open scoped Pointwise
29	
30	variable {R} [CommRing R] (r : R) (M : Type*) {M' M''}
31	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [AddCommGroup M'] [Module R M']
32	    [AddCommGroup M''] [Module R M'']
33	
34	/-- An abbreviation for `M⧸rM` that keeps us from having to write
35	`(⊤ : Submodule R M)` over and over to satisfy the typechecker. -/
36	abbrev QuotSMulTop := M ⧸ r • (⊤ : Submodule R M)
37	
38	namespace QuotSMulTop
39	
40	open Submodule Function TensorProduct
41	
42	/-- If `M'` is isomorphic to `M''` as `R`-modules, then `M'⧸rM'` is isomorphic to `M''⧸rM''`. -/
43	protected def congr (e : M' ≃ₗ[R] M'') : QuotSMulTop r M' ≃ₗ[R] QuotSMulTop r M'' :=
44	  Submodule.Quotient.equiv (r • ⊤) (r • ⊤) e <|
45	    (Submodule.map_pointwise_smul r _ e.toLinearMap).trans (by simp)
46	
47	/-- Reducing a module modulo `r` is the same as left tensoring with `R/(r)`. -/
48	noncomputable def equivQuotTensor :
49	    QuotSMulTop r M ≃ₗ[R] (R ⧸ Ideal.span {r}) ⊗[R] M :=
50	  quotEquivOfEq _ _ (ideal_span_singleton_smul _ _).symm ≪≫ₗ
51	    (quotTensorEquivQuotSMul M _).symm
52	
53	/-- Reducing a module modulo `r` is the same as right tensoring with `R/(r)`. -/
54	noncomputable def equivTensorQuot :
55	    QuotSMulTop r M ≃ₗ[R] M ⊗[R] (R ⧸ Ideal.span {r}) :=
56	  quotEquivOfEq _ _ (ideal_span_singleton_smul _ _).symm ≪≫ₗ
57	    (tensorQuotEquivQuotSMul M _).symm
58	
59	variable {M}
60	
61	/-- The action of the functor `QuotSMulTop r` on morphisms. -/
62	def map : (M →ₗ[R] M') →ₗ[R] QuotSMulTop r M →ₗ[R] QuotSMulTop r M' :=
63	  Submodule.mapQLinear _ _ ∘ₗ LinearMap.id.codRestrict _ fun _ =>
64	    map_le_iff_le_comap.mp <| le_of_eq_of_le (map_pointwise_smul _ _ _) <|
65	      smul_mono_right r le_top
66	
67	@[simp]
68	lemma map_apply_mk (f : M →ₗ[R] M') (x : M) :
69	    map r f (Submodule.Quotient.mk x) =
70	      (Submodule.Quotient.mk (f x) : QuotSMulTop r M') := rfl
71	
72	-- weirdly expensive to typecheck the type here?
73	lemma map_comp_mkQ (f : M →ₗ[R] M') :
74	    map r f ∘ₗ mkQ (r • ⊤) = mkQ (r • ⊤) ∘ₗ f := by
75	  ext; rfl
76	
77	variable (M)
78	
79	@[simp]
80	lemma map_id : map r (LinearMap.id : M →ₗ[R] M) = .id :=
81	  DFunLike.ext _ _ <| (mkQ_surjective _).forall.mpr fun _ => rfl
82	
83	variable {M}
84	
85	@[simp]
86	lemma map_comp (g : M' →ₗ[R] M'') (f : M →ₗ[R] M') :
87	    map r (g ∘ₗ f) = map r g ∘ₗ map r f :=
88	  DFunLike.ext _ _ <| (mkQ_surjective _).forall.mpr fun _ => rfl
89	
90	lemma equivQuotTensor_naturality_mk (f : M →ₗ[R] M') (x : M) :
91	    equivQuotTensor r M' (map r f (Submodule.Quotient.mk x)) =
92	      f.lTensor (R ⧸ Ideal.span {r})
93	        (equivQuotTensor r M (Submodule.Quotient.mk x)) :=
94	  (LinearMap.lTensor_tmul (R ⧸ Ideal.span {r}) f 1 x).symm
95	
96	lemma equivQuotTensor_naturality (f : M →ₗ[R] M') :
97	    equivQuotTensor r M' ∘ₗ map r f =
98	      f.lTensor (R ⧸ Ideal.span {r}) ∘ₗ equivQuotTensor r M :=
99	  quot_hom_ext _ _ _ (equivQuotTensor_naturality_mk r f)
100

277	/-!
278	### Sets acting on Submodules
279	
280	Let `R` be a (semi)ring and `M` an `R`-module. Let `S` be a monoid which acts on `M` distributively,
281	then subsets of `S` can act on submodules of `M`.
282	For subset `s ⊆ S` and submodule `N ≤ M`, we define `s • N` to be the smallest submodule containing
283	all `r • n` where `r ∈ s` and `n ∈ N`.
284	
285	#### Results
286	For arbitrary monoids `S` acting distributively on `M`, there is an induction principle for `s • N`:
287	To prove `P` holds for all `s • N`, it is enough
288	to prove:
289	- for all `r ∈ s` and `n ∈ N`, `P (r • n)`;
290	- for all `r` and `m ∈ s • N`, `P (r • n)`;
291	- for all `m₁, m₂`, `P m₁` and `P m₂` implies `P (m₁ + m₂)`;
292	- `P 0`.
293	
294	To invoke this induction principle, use `induction x, hx using Submodule.set_smul_inductionOn` where
